entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use <think> tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: <value>, y: <value> }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
## Workspace — Current Scope (no variables declared)
## Available Functions (none)
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "step1", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "step2", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Load relevant knowledge (use space name as top-level key) var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 3. Do work for step 1 var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... }
// 4. Mark step 1 complete checkpoint("main", "step1", { key: result.key })
// 5. Do work for step 2 var final = await anotherFunction() checkpoint("main", "step2", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 2. Read the loaded content with await stop() to understand it 3. Combine cooking and nutrition knowledge to give well-rounded advice 4. Display results using the React components
Available components: - display(<RecipeCard name="..." cuisine="..." method="..." servings={4} time="30 min" ingredients={[...]} steps={[...]} />) — show a recipe - display(<NutritionCard title="..." category="..." highlights={[...]} sources={[...]} />) — show nutrition info - display(<TipCard title="..." content="..." />) — show a cooking or nutrition tip - display(<MealPlanCard title="..." strategy="..." meals={[{ label: "Breakfast", description: "..." }, ...]} />) — show a meal plan - var input = await ask(<RequestForm />) — ask what the user wants help with
Example flow — loading from both spaces at once: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": true } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": true } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": true } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": true } } } }) await stop(docs) // Now combine cooking + nutrition knowledge in your response
Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

Example flow — loading from both spaces at once: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": true } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": true } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": true } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": true } } } }) await stop(docs) // Now combine cooking + nutrition knowledge in your response
Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use <think> tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: <value>, y: <value> }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.

## Available Functions (none)

italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "step1", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "step2", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Load relevant knowledge (use space name as top-level key) var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 3. Do work for step 1 var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... }
// 4. Mark step 1 complete checkpoint("main", "step1", { key: result.key })
// 5. Do work for step 2 var final = await anotherFunction() checkpoint("main", "step2", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)

Example flow — loading from both spaces at once: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": true } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": true } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": true } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": true } } } }) await stop(docs) // Now combine cooking + nutrition knowledge in your response
Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

field.name: content

field.text: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "cooking_session" incomplete. Remaining: load_knowledge, suggest_recipe. Continue from where you left off.

field.name: italian
field.text:
# Italian Cuisine
Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.
## Key Principles - **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity. - **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta. - **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time. - **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.
## Essential Techniques - **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating. - **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream. - **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.
## Staple Ingredients Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.
## Classic Dishes Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu.

field.name: vegetarian
field.text:
# Vegetarian Cooking
Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.
## Substitution Strategies - **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts - **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste - **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock - **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)
## Tips - Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth - Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat - Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star

field.name: gluten-free
field.text:
# Gluten-Free Cooking
Gluten-free cooking avoids wheat, barley, rye, and products derived from them. This is essential for people with celiac disease or gluten sensitivity.
## Substitution Strategies - **Flour** — Rice flour, almond flour, oat flour (certified GF), tapioca starch, potato starch, or commercial GF blends - **Pasta** — Rice noodles, corn pasta, chickpea pasta, zucchini noodles, sweet potato glass noodles - **Breadcrumbs** — Crushed rice crackers, almond meal, crushed cornflakes, polenta crumbs - **Thickeners** — Cornstarch or arrowroot instead of wheat flour for sauces and gravies - **Soy sauce** — Use tamari (check label for wheat-free) or coconut aminos
## Hidden Gluten Sources - Soy sauce (most brands contain wheat) - Malt vinegar - Some spice blends and bouillon cubes - Beer (use GF beer or wine for cooking) - Processed foods with "modified food starch"
## Tips - Many cuisines are naturally gluten-free: Mexican (corn-based), Japanese (rice-based), Indian (lentil and rice dishes) - When baking GF, add xanthan gum (½ tsp per cup of flour) to improve structure - Always check labels — gluten hides in unexpected places

field.name: dairy-free
field.text:
# Dairy-Free Cooking
Dairy-free cooking eliminates milk, cheese, butter, cream, yogurt, and all products containing them.
## Substitution Strategies - **Butter** — Olive oil, coconut oil, or vegan butter. For baking, use coconut oil (solid) at a 1:1 ratio. - **Milk** — Oat milk (best for cooking/baking), coconut milk (rich sauces, curries), almond milk (light uses) - **Cream** — Full-fat coconut cream, cashew cream (blend soaked cashews with water) - **Cheese** — Nutritional yeast for cheesy flavor, cashew-based sauces for creaminess, avocado for richness - **Yogurt** — Coconut yogurt or soy yogurt
## Tips - Coconut cream is the best dairy-free substitute for heavy cream in sauces — refrigerate the can and scoop the solid cream - Nutritional yeast adds a savory, cheese-like flavor to pasta, popcorn, and sauces - Many Asian cuisines (Thai, Chinese, Japanese) are naturally dairy-free and excellent references - When making a roux, use olive oil or vegan butter instead of butter — the technique is identical

field.name: saute
field.text:
# Sauté
High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.
## When to Use - Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus) - Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops) - Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing
## Key Technique 1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke. 2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed. 3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning. 4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.
## Equipment A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.
## Common Mistakes - Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing) - Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils) - Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)

field.name: braise
field.text:
# Braise
Braising combines dry heat (searing) and wet heat (simmering in liquid) for dishes that are deeply flavorful and fall-apart tender.
## When to Use - Tough, collagen-rich cuts of meat (short ribs, pork shoulder, lamb shanks, chuck roast) - Root vegetables that benefit from long cooking (carrots, turnips, parsnips) - Any dish where you want rich, concentrated sauce built from the cooking liquid
## Key Technique 1. **Sear first** — Pat the protein dry, season generously, and sear in hot oil until deeply browned on all sides. This is where most of the flavor comes from. 2. **Build aromatics** — Sauté onions, carrots, celery, and garlic in the same pot after searing. 3. **Deglaze** — Add wine, stock, or other liquid and scrape up the fond (browned bits). 4. **Submerge halfway** — The liquid should come about halfway up the protein, not cover it completely. 5. **Low and slow** — Cover and cook at 150–165°C (300–325°F) for 2–4 hours until fork-tender. 6. **Rest and reduce** — Remove the protein, strain and reduce the braising liquid into a sauce.
## Equipment A heavy Dutch oven with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enameled cast iron retains heat evenly.
## Common Mistakes - Skipping the sear (you lose enormous flavor) - Cooking at too high a temperature (the protein toughens before the collagen breaks down) - Not reducing the braising liquid (thin, watery sauce instead of a rich glaze)

field.name: grill
field.text:
# Grill
Grilling uses direct radiant heat from below to cook food quickly, creating characteristic char marks and smoky flavor.
## When to Use - Steaks, burgers, chops, and other quick-cooking proteins - Vegetables with enough structure to hold together (corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant) - Flatbreads, pizza, and fruit (peaches, pineapple)
## Key Technique 1. **Preheat thoroughly** — 15–20 minutes with the lid closed. The grates should be too hot to hold your hand near for more than 2 seconds. 2. **Clean and oil the grates** — Brush with a grill brush, then oil with a folded paper towel dipped in high-smoke-point oil. 3. **Two-zone setup** — Pile coals on one side (or turn on only half the burners) for a hot sear zone and a cooler indirect zone. 4. **Don't press** — Pressing burgers or chicken squeezes out juices. Let the food sit until it releases naturally. 5. **Rest the meat** — Rest for 5–10 minutes after grilling. Internal temperature will rise 3–5°F during resting.
## Temperature Guide - **High (230°C+ / 450°F+)** — Steaks, thin cuts, vegetables - **Medium (175–230°C / 350–450°F)** — Chicken pieces, fish, sausages - **Low indirect (120–150°C / 250–300°F)** — Whole chickens, ribs, large roasts
## Common Mistakes - Opening the lid too often (loses heat and extends cooking time) - Only using direct heat (thick cuts burn outside before cooking through) - Not letting the grill preheat (food sticks and doesn't char properly)

field.name: protein
field.text:
# Protein
Protein is made up of amino acids — 9 of which are essential and must come from food.
## Daily Targets - **Sedentary adults** — 0.8 g per kg of body weight - **Active individuals** — 1.2–1.6 g/kg - **Strength athletes** — 1.6–2.2 g/kg
## Complete vs Incomplete - **Complete proteins** contain all 9 essential amino acids: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, soy, quinoa. - **Incomplete proteins** lack one or more: beans, lentils, grains, nuts. Combine throughout the day to cover all amino acids.
## Top Sources (per 100 g) | Food | Protein | |------|---------| | Chicken breast | 31 g | | Greek yogurt | 10 g | | Lentils (cooked) | 9 g | | Eggs (2 large) | 12 g | | Tofu (firm) | 17 g |
## Timing Spreading protein across 3–4 meals (20–40 g each) maximizes muscle protein synthesis better than loading it into one meal.

field.name: carbohydrates
field.text:
# Carbohydrates
Carbohydrates are the body's preferred fuel source, especially for the brain and high-intensity exercise.
## Types - **Simple carbs** — Quick-digesting sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose). Found in fruit, honey, table sugar. - **Complex carbs** — Longer chains that digest slowly, providing steady energy. Found in whole grains, legumes, starchy vegetables. - **Fiber** — Indigestible carbs that support gut health and satiety. Soluble fiber (oats, beans) lowers cholesterol; insoluble fiber (whole wheat, vegetables) aids digestion.
## Daily Targets - **General** — 45–65% of total calories (225–325 g on a 2000 kcal diet) - **Endurance athletes** — 6–10 g/kg body weight - **Low-carb approaches** — 50–150 g/day depending on the protocol
## Glycemic Index - **Low GI (< 55)** — Lentils, most fruits, steel-cut oats. Steady blood sugar. - **Medium GI (56–69)** — Brown rice, whole wheat bread, sweet potato. - **High GI (> 70)** — White bread, white rice, potatoes. Fast energy but rapid blood sugar spike.
## Top Sources Oats, brown rice, sweet potatoes, quinoa, beans, whole-grain bread, bananas, berries.

field.name: fats
field.text:
# Fats
Dietary fat is calorie-dense (9 kcal/g vs 4 kcal/g for protein and carbs) but essential for hormone production, brain function, and absorbing fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
## Types - **Unsaturated (healthy)** — Monounsaturated (olive oil, avocado) and polyunsaturated (fish, walnuts, flaxseed). Lower inflammation and support heart health. - **Saturated** — Solid at room temperature (butter, coconut oil, red meat fat). Limit to < 10% of calories. - **Trans fats** — Industrially hydrogenated oils. Avoid entirely — linked to heart disease. - **Omega-3 fatty acids** — EPA and DHA (fatty fish) and ALA (flaxseed, chia). Anti-inflammatory, critical for brain health. Aim for 2 servings of fatty fish per week.
## Daily Targets - **General** — 20–35% of total calories (44–78 g on a 2000 kcal diet) - **Minimum** — Do not go below 15% of calories; hormonal disruption can occur.
## Top Sources Olive oil, avocado, salmon, sardines, walnuts, almonds, chia seeds, eggs, dark chocolate (70%+).

field.name: plate-method
field.text:
# The Plate Method
A simple, no-counting approach to balanced meals. Divide your plate visually:
## The Template - **1/2 plate — Non-starchy vegetables** — Leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, zucchini, cauliflower. Volume and fiber for satiety. - **1/4 plate — Lean protein** — Chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, legumes. Palm-sized portion (~20–30 g protein). - **1/4 plate — Complex carbs** — Brown rice, sweet potato, quinoa, whole-grain bread. Fist-sized portion. - **Thumb of healthy fat** — Olive oil drizzle, avocado slice, handful of nuts.
## Benefits - No calorie counting or food scales required - Works at restaurants and social meals - Naturally portion-controlled (~500–600 kcal per plate) - Easy to teach to families and children
## Adjustments - **Weight loss** — Increase vegetables to 2/3, reduce carb portion - **Athletes** — Increase carb and protein portions proportionally - **Breakfast** — Same principle: eggs (protein) + oats (carb) + berries (veg/fruit) + nut butter (fat)

field.name: batch-prep
field.text:
# Batch Prep
Dedicate 2–3 hours once or twice a week to prepare components that mix-and-match into complete meals.
## Core Components to Prep 1. **2–3 proteins** — Grill chicken, bake salmon, cook a pot of lentils 2. **2–3 grains/starches** — Rice, quinoa, roasted sweet potatoes 3. **3–4 vegetables** — Roast a sheet pan of mixed veg, prep raw salad ingredients, steam broccoli 4. **Sauces/dressings** — Tahini dressing, chimichurri, simple vinaigrette
## Storage Guidelines - **Fridge** — Cooked proteins and grains last 3–4 days. Store in airtight containers. - **Freezer** — Soups, stews, and cooked grains freeze well for 2–3 months. Portion into single servings. - **Fresh components** — Wash and chop vegetables, but dress salads only when serving.
## Weekly Template | Day | Lunch | Dinner | |-----|-------|--------| | Mon | Chicken + quinoa + roasted veg | Salmon + rice + steamed broccoli | | Tue | Lentil bowl + sweet potato | Chicken stir-fry + rice | | Wed | Salmon salad + quinoa | Lentil soup (from freezer) | | Thu | Chicken wrap + raw veg | Fresh cook night | | Fri | Leftovers bowl | Flexible / eat out |
## Tips - Invest in quality glass containers (different sizes) - Label everything with date - Prep while something is in the oven — multitask

field.name: calorie-tracking
field.text:
# Calorie Tracking
A quantitative approach to nutrition — set daily calorie and macronutrient targets, then track intake against them.
## Setting Your Target 1. **Calculate TDEE** (Total Daily Energy Expenditure): - BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) x Activity Multiplier - Sedentary: BMR x 1.2 | Lightly active: x 1.375 | Active: x 1.55 | Very active: x 1.725 2. **Adjust for goal**: - **Fat loss** — Subtract 300–500 kcal (moderate deficit) - **Maintenance** — Eat at TDEE - **Muscle gain** — Add 200–300 kcal (lean bulk)
## Macro Split (Starting Point) - **Balanced** — 30% protein / 40% carbs / 30% fat - **High protein** — 35% protein / 35% carbs / 30% fat - **Endurance** — 25% protein / 50% carbs / 25% fat
## Practical Tips - Weigh food raw (cooked weight varies) - Front-load protein — plan protein sources first, fill in carbs and fats - Don't aim for perfection — within 50–100 kcal of target is fine - Track for 2–4 weeks to build intuition, then transition to intuitive eating if desired
## Common Pitfalls - Forgetting cooking oils (1 tbsp = 120 kcal) - Ignoring liquid calories (lattes, juice, alcohol) - Weekend "cheat days" that erase the weekly deficit - Obsessive tracking leading to disordered eating — take breaks if it feels unhealthy

entry.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use <think> tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: <value>, y: <value> }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
## Workspace — Current Scope Name Type Value ----------- ------ -------------------------------------------------- preferences Object {request, dietary} docs Object {cooking, nutrition}
## Available Functions (none)
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "step1", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "step2", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Load relevant knowledge (use space name as top-level key) var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 3. Do work for step 1 var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... }
// 4. Mark step 1 complete checkpoint("main", "step1", { key: result.key })
// 5. Do work for step 2 var final = await anotherFunction() checkpoint("main", "step2", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 2. Read the loaded content with await stop() to understand it 3. Combine cooking and nutrition knowledge to give well-rounded advice 4. Display results using the React components
Available components: - display(<RecipeCard name="..." cuisine="..." method="..." servings={4} time="30 min" ingredients={[...]} steps={[...]} />) — show a recipe - display(<NutritionCard title="..." category="..." highlights={[...]} sources={[...]} />) — show nutrition info - display(<TipCard title="..." content="..." />) — show a cooking or nutrition tip - display(<MealPlanCard title="..." strategy="..." meals={[{ label: "Breakfast", description: "..." }, ...]} />) — show a meal plan - var input = await ask(<RequestForm />) — ask what the user wants help with
Example flow — loading from both spaces at once: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": true } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": true } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": true } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": true } } } }) await stop(docs) // Now combine cooking + nutrition knowledge in your response
Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.

field.name: content
field.text:
← stop { docs: { "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": "# Italian Cuisine

Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.

## Key Principles
- **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity.
- **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta.
- **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time.
- **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.

## Essential Techniques
- **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating.
- **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream.
- **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.

## Staple Ingredients
Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.

## Classic Dishes
Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu." } }, "dietary": { "restriction": { "vegetarian": "# Vegetarian Cooking

Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts
- **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste
- **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock
- **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)

## Tips
- Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth
- Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat
- Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star", "gluten-free": "# Gluten-Free Cooking

Gluten-free cooking avoids wheat, barley, rye, and products derived from them. This is essential for people with celiac disease or gluten sensitivity.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Flour** — Rice flour, almond flour, oat flour (certified GF), tapioca starch, potato starch, or commercial GF blends
- **Pasta** — Rice noodles, corn pasta, chickpea pasta, zucchini noodles, sweet potato glass noodles
- **Breadcrumbs** — Crushed rice crackers, almond meal, crushed cornflakes, polenta crumbs
- **Thickeners** — Cornstarch or arrowroot instead of wheat flour for sauces and gravies
- **Soy sauce** — Use tamari (check label for wheat-free) or coconut aminos

## Hidden Gluten Sources
- Soy sauce (most brands contain wheat)
- Malt vinegar
- Some spice blends and bouillon cubes
- Beer (use GF beer or wine for cooking)
- Processed foods with "modified food starch"

## Tips
- Many cuisines are naturally gluten-free: Mexican (corn-based), Japanese (rice-based), Indian (lentil and rice dishes)
- When baking GF, add xanthan gum (½ tsp per cup of flour) to improve structure
- Always check labels — gluten hides in unexpected places", "dairy-free": "# Dairy-Free Cooking

Dairy-free cooking eliminates milk, cheese, butter, cream, yogurt, and all products containing them.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Butter** — Olive oil, coconut oil, or vegan butter. For baking, use coconut oil (solid) at a 1:1 ratio.
- **Milk** — Oat milk (best for cooking/baking), coconut milk (rich sauces, curries), almond milk (light uses)
- **Cream** — Full-fat coconut cream, cashew cream (blend soaked cashews with water)
- **Cheese** — Nutritional yeast for cheesy flavor, cashew-based sauces for creaminess, avocado for richness
- **Yogurt** — Coconut yogurt or soy yogurt

## Tips
- Coconut cream is the best dairy-free substitute for heavy cream in sauces — refrigerate the can and scoop the solid cream
- Nutritional yeast adds a savory, cheese-like flavor to pasta, popcorn, and sauces
- Many Asian cuisines (Thai, Chinese, Japanese) are naturally dairy-free and excellent references
- When making a roux, use olive oil or vegan butter instead of butter — the technique is identical" } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté

High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.

## When to Use
- Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus)
- Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops)
- Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing

## Key Technique
1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.
2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed.
3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning.
4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.

## Equipment
A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.

## Common Mistakes
- Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing)
- Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils)
- Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)", "braise": "# Braise

Braising combines dry heat (searing) and wet heat (simmering in liquid) for dishes that are deeply flavorful and fall-apart tender.

## When to Use
- Tough, collagen-rich cuts of meat (short ribs, pork shoulder, lamb shanks, chuck roast)
- Root vegetables that benefit from long cooking (carrots, turnips, parsnips)
- Any dish where you want rich, concentrated sauce built from the cooking liquid

## Key Technique
1. **Sear first** — Pat the protein dry, season generously, and sear in hot oil until deeply browned on all sides. This is where most of the flavor comes from.
2. **Build aromatics** — Sauté onions, carrots, celery, and garlic in the same pot after searing.
3. **Deglaze** — Add wine, stock, or other liquid and scrape up the fond (browned bits).
4. **Submerge halfway** — The liquid should come about halfway up the protein, not cover it completely.
5. **Low and slow** — Cover and cook at 150–165°C (300–325°F) for 2–4 hours until fork-tender.
6. **Rest and reduce** — Remove the protein, strain and reduce the braising liquid into a sauce.

## Equipment
A heavy Dutch oven with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enameled cast iron retains heat evenly.

## Common Mistakes
- Skipping the sear (you lose enormous flavor)
- Cooking at too high a temperature (the protein toughens before the collagen breaks down)
- Not reducing the braising liquid (thin, watery sauce instead of a rich glaze)", "grill": "# Grill

Grilling uses direct radiant heat from below to cook food quickly, creating characteristic char marks and smoky flavor.

## When to Use
- Steaks, burgers, chops, and other quick-cooking proteins
- Vegetables with enough structure to hold together (corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant)
- Flatbreads, pizza, and fruit (peaches, pineapple)

## Key Technique
1. **Preheat thoroughly** — 15–20 minutes with the lid closed. The grates should be too hot to hold your hand near for more than 2 seconds.
2. **Clean and oil the grates** — Brush with a grill brush, then oil with a folded paper towel dipped in high-smoke-point oil.
3. **Two-zone setup** — Pile coals on one side (or turn on only half the burners) for a hot sear zone and a cooler indirect zone.
4. **Don't press** — Pressing burgers or chicken squeezes out juices. Let the food sit until it releases naturally.
5. **Rest the meat** — Rest for 5–10 minutes after grilling. Internal temperature will rise 3–5°F during resting.

## Temperature Guide
- **High (230°C+ / 450°F+)** — Steaks, thin cuts, vegetables
- **Medium (175–230°C / 350–450°F)** — Chicken pieces, fish, sausages
- **Low indirect (120–150°C / 250–300°F)** — Whole chickens, ribs, large roasts

## Common Mistakes
- Opening the lid too often (loses heat and extends cooking time)
- Only using direct heat (thick cuts burn outside before cooking through)
- Not letting the grill preheat (food sticks and doesn't char properly)" } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": "# Protein

Protein is made up of amino acids — 9 of which are essential and must come from food.

## Daily Targets
- **Sedentary adults** — 0.8 g per kg of body weight
- **Active individuals** — 1.2–1.6 g/kg
- **Strength athletes** — 1.6–2.2 g/kg

## Complete vs Incomplete
- **Complete proteins** contain all 9 essential amino acids: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, soy, quinoa.
- **Incomplete proteins** lack one or more: beans, lentils, grains, nuts. Combine throughout the day to cover all amino acids.

## Top Sources (per 100 g)
| Food | Protein |
|------|---------|
| Chicken breast | 31 g |
| Greek yogurt | 10 g |
| Lentils (cooked) | 9 g |
| Eggs (2 large) | 12 g |
| Tofu (firm) | 17 g |

## Timing
Spreading protein across 3–4 meals (20–40 g each) maximizes muscle protein synthesis better than loading it into one meal.", "carbohydrates": "# Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates are the body's preferred fuel source, especially for the brain and high-intensity exercise.

## Types
- **Simple carbs** — Quick-digesting sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose). Found in fruit, honey, table sugar.
- **Complex carbs** — Longer chains that digest slowly, providing steady energy. Found in whole grains, legumes, starchy vegetables.
- **Fiber** — Indigestible carbs that support gut health and satiety. Soluble fiber (oats, beans) lowers cholesterol; insoluble fiber (whole wheat, vegetables) aids digestion.

## Daily Targets
- **General** — 45–65% of total calories (225–325 g on a 2000 kcal diet)
- **Endurance athletes** — 6–10 g/kg body weight
- **Low-carb approaches** — 50–150 g/day depending on the protocol

## Glycemic Index
- **Low GI (< 55)** — Lentils, most fruits, steel-cut oats. Steady blood sugar.
- **Medium GI (56–69)** — Brown rice, whole wheat bread, sweet potato.
- **High GI (> 70)** — White bread, white rice, potatoes. Fast energy but rapid blood sugar spike.

## Top Sources
Oats, brown rice, sweet potatoes, quinoa, beans, whole-grain bread, bananas, berries.", "fats": "# Fats

Dietary fat is calorie-dense (9 kcal/g vs 4 kcal/g for protein and carbs) but essential for hormone production, brain function, and absorbing fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

## Types
- **Unsaturated (healthy)** — Monounsaturated (olive oil, avocado) and polyunsaturated (fish, walnuts, flaxseed). Lower inflammation and support heart health.
- **Saturated** — Solid at room temperature (butter, coconut oil, red meat fat). Limit to < 10% of calories.
- **Trans fats** — Industrially hydrogenated oils. Avoid entirely — linked to heart disease.
- **Omega-3 fatty acids** — EPA and DHA (fatty fish) and ALA (flaxseed, chia). Anti-inflammatory, critical for brain health. Aim for 2 servings of fatty fish per week.

## Daily Targets
- **General** — 20–35% of total calories (44–78 g on a 2000 kcal diet)
- **Minimum** — Do not go below 15% of calories; hormonal disruption can occur.

## Top Sources
Olive oil, avocado, salmon, sardines, walnuts, almonds, chia seeds, eggs, dark chocolate (70%+)." } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": "# The Plate Method

A simple, no-counting approach to balanced meals. Divide your plate visually:

## The Template
- **1/2 plate — Non-starchy vegetables** — Leafy greens, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, zucchini, cauliflower. Volume and fiber for satiety.
- **1/4 plate — Lean protein** — Chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, legumes. Palm-sized portion (~20–30 g protein).
- **1/4 plate — Complex carbs** — Brown rice, sweet potato, quinoa, whole-grain bread. Fist-sized portion.
- **Thumb of healthy fat** — Olive oil drizzle, avocado slice, handful of nuts.

## Benefits
- No calorie counting or food scales required
- Works at restaurants and social meals
- Naturally portion-controlled (~500–600 kcal per plate)
- Easy to teach to families and children

## Adjustments
- **Weight loss** — Increase vegetables to 2/3, reduce carb portion
- **Athletes** — Increase carb and protein portions proportionally
- **Breakfast** — Same principle: eggs (protein) + oats (carb) + berries (veg/fruit) + nut butter (fat)", "batch-prep": "# Batch Prep

Dedicate 2–3 hours once or twice a week to prepare components that mix-and-match into complete meals.

## Core Components to Prep
1. **2–3 proteins** — Grill chicken, bake salmon, cook a pot of lentils
2. **2–3 grains/starches** — Rice, quinoa, roasted sweet potatoes
3. **3–4 vegetables** — Roast a sheet pan of mixed veg, prep raw salad ingredients, steam broccoli
4. **Sauces/dressings** — Tahini dressing, chimichurri, simple vinaigrette

## Storage Guidelines
- **Fridge** — Cooked proteins and grains last 3–4 days. Store in airtight containers.
- **Freezer** — Soups, stews, and cooked grains freeze well for 2–3 months. Portion into single servings.
- **Fresh components** — Wash and chop vegetables, but dress salads only when serving.

## Weekly Template
| Day | Lunch | Dinner |
|-----|-------|--------|
| Mon | Chicken + quinoa + roasted veg | Salmon + rice + steamed broccoli |
| Tue | Lentil bowl + sweet potato | Chicken stir-fry + rice |
| Wed | Salmon salad + quinoa | Lentil soup (from freezer) |
| Thu | Chicken wrap + raw veg | Fresh cook night |
| Fri | Leftovers bowl | Flexible / eat out |

## Tips
- Invest in quality glass containers (different sizes)
- Label everything with date
- Prep while something is in the oven — multitask", "calorie-tracking": "# Calorie Tracking

A quantitative approach to nutrition — set daily calorie and macronutrient targets, then track intake against them.

## Setting Your Target
1. **Calculate TDEE** (Total Daily Energy Expenditure):
 - BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) x Activity Multiplier
 - Sedentary: BMR x 1.2 | Lightly active: x 1.375 | Active: x 1.55 | Very active: x 1.725
2. **Adjust for goal**:
 - **Fat loss** — Subtract 300–500 kcal (moderate deficit)
 - **Maintenance** — Eat at TDEE
 - **Muscle gain** — Add 200–300 kcal (lean bulk)

## Macro Split (Starting Point)
- **Balanced** — 30% protein / 40% carbs / 30% fat
- **High protein** — 35% protein / 35% carbs / 30% fat
- **Endurance** — 25% protein / 50% carbs / 25% fat

## Practical Tips
- Weigh food raw (cooked weight varies)
- Front-load protein — plan protein sources first, fill in carbs and fats
- Don't aim for perfection — within 50–100 kcal of target is fine
- Track for 2–4 weeks to build intuition, then transition to intuitive eating if desired

## Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting cooking oils (1 tbsp = 120 kcal)
- Ignoring liquid calories (lattes, juice, alcohol)
- Weekend "cheat days" that erase the weekly deficit
- Obsessive tracking leading to disordered eating — take breaks if it feels unhealthy" } } } } }

field.text: "Bring a large pot of salted water to boil. Cook spaghetti al dente, reserving 1 cup pasta water before draining.",

field.name: lines

item.text: Bring a large pot of salted water to boil. Cook spaghetti al dente, reserving 1 cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, sauté pancetta in a pan until crispy (about 5 minutes). Remove from heat.
In a bowl, whisk together eggs, most of the Pecorino, and plenty of black pepper.
Working quickly, add hot drained pasta to the pan with pancetta. Remove from heat.
Pour egg mixture over pasta, tossing vigorously to create a creamy sauce. Add pasta water as needed.
Serve immediately topped with remaining Pecorino and more black pepper.

field.name: lines
field.text: content="The key to creamy carbonara is technique, not cream. Remove the pan from heat before adding eggs, and toss constantly to prevent scrambling. The residual heat cooks the eggs perfectly while the starchy pasta water creates a silky sauce."

field.name: content
field.text:
You are a code-execution agent. You respond EXCLUSIVELY with valid TypeScript code. No markdown. No prose. No explanations outside of code comments. Every character you emit is fed line-by-line into a live TypeScript REPL that executes as you stream.
## Execution Model
Your output is NOT a script that runs after you finish. Each line is parsed and executed as it arrives. Think of yourself as typing into a live terminal.
The REPL supports top-level await. Every async function call must be awaited.
CRITICAL: Do NOT wrap code in markdown fences (```). Output raw TypeScript only. Do NOT use <think> tags or any XML tags.
## Available Globals
### await stop(...values) — Pause and read Suspends your execution. The runtime evaluates each argument, serializes the results, and injects them as a user message prefixed with "← stop". You resume with knowledge of those values.
Use stop when you need to inspect a runtime value before deciding what to write next. Example: await stop(x, y) → you will see: ← stop { x: <value>, y: <value> }
IMPORTANT: After calling await stop(), STOP writing code. The runtime will pause your stream, read the values, and resume you in a new turn. Do NOT predict or simulate the stop response yourself.
### display(element) — Show output to user Non-blocking. Appends a rendered component to the user's view.
### checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) — Declare a task plan with milestones Before starting any implementation work, declare a plan using checkpoints(). This registers milestones with the host under a unique tasklistId. Each checkpoint has an id, instructions, and outputSchema describing the result shape.
You can call checkpoints() multiple times per session with different tasklist IDs. It does not block execution.
Example: checkpoints("analyze_data", "Analyze employee data", [ { id: "load", instructions: "Load the dataset", outputSchema: { count: { type: "number" } } }, { id: "analyze", instructions: "Compute statistics", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } }, { id: "report", instructions: "Present results", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
### checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) — Mark a milestone as complete When you reach a milestone, call checkpoint() with the tasklist ID, checkpoint ID, and an output object matching the declared outputSchema. Non-blocking. Must be called in declaration order within each tasklist — do not skip checkpoints.
Example: checkpoint("analyze_data", "load", { count: 10 })
If your stream ends before all checkpoints are complete, the host will send you a reminder: ⚠ [system] Tasklist "analyze_data" incomplete. Remaining: analyze, report. Continue from where you left off. When you see this, continue from the next incomplete checkpoint. Do NOT re-declare checkpoints() for the same tasklist or redo completed work.
### loadKnowledge(selector) — Load knowledge files from spaces Loads markdown content from the knowledge base. Pass a selector object that mirrors the knowledge tree structure, setting `true` on the specific files you want to load.
The selector uses space names as the top-level keys, matching the Knowledge Tree structure: `{ spaceName: { domain: { field: { option: true } } } }`
Returns an object with the same structure, where each `true` is replaced with the markdown content of that file.
Example: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "my-space": { "chat-modes": { "mode": { "casual": true } } } }) // docs["my-space"]["chat-modes"]["mode"]["casual"] → "# Casual Mode\n\nRelaxed, conversational..."
Use the Knowledge Tree below to see what spaces and files are available. Load only the specific files relevant to the current task — NEVER load an entire domain or space at once. Select individual options that match the user's request.
## Workspace — Current Scope Name Type Value ----------- ------ -------------------------------------------------- preferences Object {request, dietary} docs Object {cooking, nutrition}
## Available Functions (none)
## Knowledge Tree knowledge/ ├── cooking/ │ ├── cuisine/ 🌍 Cuisine — World cuisine traditions and their core techniques │ │ └── type [select] cuisineType — Which cuisine tradition to draw from │ │ ├── italian Italian — Mediterranean cooking with emphasis on fresh ingredients and simplicity │ │ ├── japanese Japanese — Precision-driven cooking emphasizing umami, presentation, and seasonal harmony │ │ └── mexican Mexican — Bold, layered flavors built on chiles, corn, and slow-cooked techniques │ ├── dietary/ 🥗 Dietary Needs — Dietary restrictions and how to adapt recipes for them │ │ └── restriction [multiSelect] dietaryRestriction — Which dietary restrictions to accommodate │ │ ├── vegetarian Vegetarian — No meat or fish, but allows dairy and eggs │ │ ├── gluten-free Gluten-Free — No wheat, barley, rye, or their derivatives │ │ └── dairy-free Dairy-Free — No milk, cheese, butter, cream, or other dairy products │ └── technique/ 🔥 Cooking Techniques — Core cooking methods and when to use them │ └── method [multiSelect] cookingMethod — Which cooking method to use │ ├── saute Sauté — Quick cooking over high heat with a small amount of fat │ ├── braise Braise — Low and slow cooking in liquid for tender, flavorful results │ └── grill Grill — Direct high-heat cooking over flame or coals for smoky, charred flavor └── nutrition/ ├── macronutrients/ 💪 Macronutrients — Protein, carbohydrates, and fats — roles, sources, and daily targets │ └── type [select] macroType — Which macronutrient to learn about │ ├── protein Protein — Building blocks for muscle, enzymes, and immune function │ ├── carbohydrates Carbohydrates — Primary energy source for brain and muscles │ └── fats Fats — Essential for hormones, cell membranes, and nutrient absorption ├── meal-planning/ 📋 Meal Planning — Strategies for balanced meals, portion control, and weekly prep │ └── strategy [select] planStrategy — Which meal planning approach to use │ ├── plate-method Plate Method — Simple visual approach — divide your plate into balanced portions │ ├── batch-prep Batch Prep — Cook once, eat all week — efficient meal preparation strategy │ └── calorie-tracking Calorie Tracking — Precise energy balance approach using calorie and macro targets └── vitamins/ 🧬 Vitamins & Minerals — Essential micronutrients, deficiency signs, and food sources └── nutrient [select] nutrient — Which vitamin or mineral to learn about ├── vitamin-d Vitamin D — The sunshine vitamin — bone health, immunity, and mood regulation ├── iron Iron — Oxygen transport, energy production, and immune defense └── b12 Vitamin B12 — Nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis
## Rules 1. Output ONLY valid TypeScript. No markdown. No prose outside // comments. 2. Plan before you build — call checkpoints(tasklistId, description, tasks) to declare milestones, then call checkpoint(tasklistId, checkpointId, output) as you complete each one. 3. Await every async call: var x = await fn() 4. Use stop() to read runtime values before branching. 5. Do not use console.log — use stop() to inspect values. 6. Do not import modules. Do not use export. 7. Use var for all declarations (not const/let) so they persist in the REPL scope across turns. 8. Handle nullability with ?. and ?? 9. After calling await stop(...), STOP. Do not write any more code until you receive the stop response. 10. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge files before starting domain-specific work. Check the Knowledge Tree to see what is available. NEVER load all files from a domain or space — only select the specific options that are relevant to the user's request. Loading too much wastes context and degrades your performance.
## Execution Flow Pattern
A typical interaction follows this pattern:
// 1. Plan — always start with checkpoints checkpoints("main", "Do the task", [ { id: "step1", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { key: { type: "string" } } }, { id: "step2", instructions: "...", outputSchema: { done: { type: "boolean" } } } ])
// 2. Load relevant knowledge (use space name as top-level key) var knowledge = loadKnowledge({ "space-name": { "domain": { "field": { "option": true } } } }) await stop(knowledge) // ← stop { knowledge: { "space-name": { domain: { field: { option: "..." } } } } }
// 3. Do work for step 1 var result = await someFunction() await stop(result) // ← stop { result: ... }
// 4. Mark step 1 complete checkpoint("main", "step1", { key: result.key })
// 5. Do work for step 2 var final = await anotherFunction() checkpoint("main", "step2", { done: true })
## Special Instructions You are a cooking and nutrition assistant powered by a multi-space knowledge base. Your knowledge tree spans two spaces:
- **Cooking** — cuisine traditions, cooking techniques, and dietary restrictions - **Nutrition** — macronutrients (protein, carbs, fats), vitamins & minerals (D, iron, B12), and meal planning strategies (plate method, batch prep, calorie tracking)
When the user asks for help: 1. Use loadKnowledge() to load relevant knowledge from BOTH spaces as needed 2. Read the loaded content with await stop() to understand it 3. Combine cooking and nutrition knowledge to give well-rounded advice 4. Display results using the React components
Available components: - display(<RecipeCard name="..." cuisine="..." method="..." servings={4} time="30 min" ingredients={[...]} steps={[...]} />) — show a recipe - display(<NutritionCard title="..." category="..." highlights={[...]} sources={[...]} />) — show nutrition info - display(<TipCard title="..." content="..." />) — show a cooking or nutrition tip - display(<MealPlanCard title="..." strategy="..." meals={[{ label: "Breakfast", description: "..." }, ...]} />) — show a meal plan - var input = await ask(<RequestForm />) — ask what the user wants help with
Example flow — loading from both spaces at once: var docs = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": true } }, "technique": { "method": { "saute": true } } }, "nutrition": { "macronutrients": { "type": { "protein": true } }, "meal-planning": { "strategy": { "plate-method": true } } } }) await stop(docs) // Now combine cooking + nutrition knowledge in your response
Always load knowledge BEFORE giving advice — don't make things up when the knowledge base has the answer. Never load all files from a space — only load the specific options relevant to the user's question.
